titleStmt.title: A Theoretical Analysis of Superconducting Pairing in Correlated Metallic Systems

affiliation.orgName: S N Bose National Centre for Basic Sciences Block-JD; Sector-III
S N Bose National Centre for Basic Sciences Block-JD; Sector-III

analytic.title: A Theoretical Analysis of Superconducting Pairing in Correlated Metallic Systems

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: We have introduced a Coulomb correlated normal state to study the superconducting pairing within a Fermi liquid(FL)-like background in a 3-dimensional system. The role of the Coulomb correlation has been actively incorporated by means of the Gutzwiller projection scheme, in the presence of phonon mediated attractive electron-electron(AEE) interaction throughout the entire regime of its applicability. We variationally modulate the Coulomb correlation strength from the weak to the strong regime in our calculational procedure. The main highlight of our results is the appearance of a '2-gap-like' structure in the superconducting phase, arising out of the two-body interacting matrix elements, calculated in the presence of the active Coulomb correlation. We have made use of the 'two-square well model' potential to evaluate the superconducting pairing gaps at zero temperature. Our Calculational scheme has been applied to a simple cubic lattice system for a physical realization. All the consequences are elaborated and discussed.

div.head: I. INTRODUCTION
div.p: Superconductivity is one of the most mysterious physical phenomena discovered in the last century. Even today, it is a challenge to prepare a stable room temperature superconducting (RTSC) system. Meanwhile, in the last year, Dias et.al. had found a superconducting state at a temperature of 287.8K in a pressure driven carbonaceous sulfur hydride system [1]. The other high-temperature superconductors (HTSC), with a critical temperature(T c ) close to the ambient temperature, were mainly found in the pressure induced superhydride systems (e.g. LH 10 , Y H x ) [2][3][4][5][6]. Their promisingly high T c , is due to the electron-electron attractive coupling mediated by the high frequency phonon which is usually generated in the materials having elements with very low atomic masses [7][8][9][10][11][12][13]. The original microscopic theory of Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer (BCS) had proposed pairing from phonon mediated electron-electron attraction [14][15][16]. The role of the repulsive Coulomb correlation in the pairing theory was analyzed within the BCS framework much later [17]. However, our aim here is to examine the superconducting pair formation in the s-wave channel from a Coulomb correlated parental normal phase itself. This will bring out up to what strength of the Coulomb correlation in the normalphase, the system can sustain superconductivity. In the recent past, R. E. Zillich et.al. have reported the BCS pair formation in a correlated low-density Fermi gas using a square-well potential as well as Lennard-Jones (LJ) potential [18]. They defined a correlated variational state to take into account the Coulomb correlation, as this scheme was previously implemented to study correlated superfluid He 3 [19][20][21]. In literature, the competition between the phonon driven AEE interaction and the Coulomb correlation was reported earlier in Holstein-Hubbard (HH) model for different lattice systems [22][23][24][25]. The Gutzwiller approximated projection scheme as was earlier proposed by M. C. Gutzwiller to study the ferromagnetism in the itinerant d-band electrons in rare-earth oxide materials, is a projected Slater determinant that minimizes the real space configuration with the prevention of doubly occupied sites. [26][27][28]. The approximated Gutzwiller wave function has been extensively studied in different lattice dimensions and therefore also been implemented to study superconductivity using the Hubbard model with an attractive interaction [29][30][31][32][33]. Meanwhile, we have studied the inter-playing characteristics of the phonon mediated AEE interaction and the Coulomb interaction in a superconductor with both weakly and strongly correlated situations [34]. We had proposed two distinct ways to construct the correlated variational state and one of which was elaborated in our previous work [34]. In ref. [34], the correlated pairing state was configured with the BCS paring operator acting on the Fermi sea (FS) ground state to form a paired state and then the Gutzwiller operator acting on the paired state. In other words, the Coulomb correlation involves the paired states. Subsequently, we put forward another way to construct the correlated BCS (CBCS) pairing state. The variational pairing state is set up starting with a correlation induced FS ground state i.e. correlated Fermi sea (CFS) ground state. The Gutzwiller partial projection operator with a variational parameter (α) takes care of the The Gutzwiller wave function in detail, is a variational wave function for many particles system that describes interacting particles on a rigid lattice systems with broken ground state symmetry (e.g. Nel state for the antiferromagnetic system). By considering this constraint as well as the approach of the introduction of the Coulomb correlation, we need to define the variational CBCS state for a real lattice system and then take it into the Fourier space to study the isotropic superconducting pairing mechanism in the presence of the active Coulomb correlation. Our approach to define the correlated fermion pairing state i.e. correlated BCS (CBCS) state is-(i) first, construct the Fermi sea state for correlated fermions i.e. correlated Fermi sea(CFS) ground state; (ii) then project out the paired particle states for BCS pairing. Hence, the CBCS state is defined as
div.formula: Ψ CBCS = P BCS ⊗ P G ψ F S(1)
div.p: where, the P BCS and P G are the BCS pairing operator and the Gutzwiller projection operator respectively. The operator P BCS is acting on the Gutzwiller projected out state from the FS ground state(ψ F S ). The CFS state is defined by making use of the Gutzwiller projection operator as
div.formula: Ψ CF S = P G ψ F S(2)
div.p: where, the Gutzwiller projection operator is defined as [29,[36][37][38]]
div.formula: P G = l (1 -αn l↑ n l↓ )(3)
div.p: where, l is the site index and n lσ is the occupation number operator at that site with spin states σ = {↑, ↓}.
The projection operator P G is preventing the electrons to sit on the same site and this double occupancy is variationally prevented through a parameter α (where, 0 α 1).
The FS ground state is defined as
div.formula: ψ F S = k F k i,j e i(ri-rj ).k c + i,σ c + j,-σ | 0 = k F k i,j e i(ri-rj ).k | iσ, j -σ(4)
div.p: where, c + i,σ (c i,σ ) is the fermion creation (annihilation) operator on the i-th site with spin state σ = {↑, ↓}. The FS is thus defined by pairwise selection of electrons with opposite momenta and spin states. Therefore, the CFS state is defined as
div.formula: Ψ CF S = l (1 -αn l↑ n l↓ ) k F k i,j e i(ri-rj ).k | iσ, j -σ (5)
div.p: On operating the BCS pairing operator on the CFS state, the CBCS state is defined as
div.formula: Ψ CBCS = |k| k F k (u k + v k f,g e i(r f -rg).k c + f,↑ c + g,↓ ) l (1 -αn l↑ n l↓ ) k F k i,j e i(ri-rj ).k | iσ, j -σ(6)
div.p: where, u k and v k are the Bogoliubov coefficients and they follow the conditions for every momentum state k, as

The CBCS state is not normalized yet, but the normalized state will be taken into account for all the analytical consequences. Meanwhile, an appendix (Appendix-A) with a calculation for the normalization constant is added with this manuscript at the end, as stated before.

div.head: B. Model Hamiltonian
div.p: In one of our previous works on the interplaying aspects of pairing correlation and the Coulomb correlation in a phonon mediated superconductor, we have made use of the reduced zero temperature BCS Hamiltonian [14,34]. However, we aim to study a more realistic electron system by setting up a variational state as defined in Eq. (6). In this scenario, it is obvious to define the corresponding Hamiltonian in real space by going over to the Fourier space. Therefore, the real space realization of zero temperature reduced BCS Hamiltonian on a simple cubic lattice for illustration, can now be written as
div.formula: H = i,j 2ε ij b + i b j + i ,j V i j b + i b j(8)
div.p: where, ε ij = ε 0 -2t(cosk x a+cosk y a+cosk z a) is the single particle tight binding(TB) energy dispersion for 3D simple cubic lattice, considering nearest neighbour hopping only [39]. Here, ε 0 , t and a are the site energy, nearest neighbour hopping amplitude and the lattice parameter respectively
div.formula: + k = c + k↑ c + -k↓ ; b k = c -k↓ c k↑ .
div.p: It may be worthwhile remarking here that several superconductors like Sn -T e, Sn -Sb, T e -Au, Au x Sb 1-x-y T e y possess simple cubic structures both in the normal and superconducting phases [40][41][42][43].
The first term within the above Hamiltonian, is representing the paired particle hopping energy, whereas
div.formula: V i j = kk V kk e -i(k.r i -k .r j )
div.p: is the two-body interacting potential. The quantity V kk is the matrix element used in the BCS formalism to connect two different paired momentum states k and k . Beforehand, we do not choose any specific form of the potential; however for our interacting system it is clear that both attractive and the repulsive nature of interactions will appear.

div.head: A. Evaluation of ground state energy and Pairing gap functions
div.p: The ground state energy expression can be evaluated by combining Eqs. [6] and [8] as
div.formula: W = Ψ CBCS |H|Ψ CBCS Ψ CBCS |Ψ CBCS(9)
div.p: On evaluating the ground state energy in the CBCS state, we find

where, the first term corresponds to the particle-pair energy as measured in the CBCS ground state and the second one represents the interacting matrix elements for the interacting potential. The matrix element P k,k which in general connects two different paired momentum states k and k , replaces bare two-body matrix element as prescribed in the usual BCS formalism. The expression for P k,k in our case can be cast in the following form:
div.formula: P k,k = (1 + α 2 ) Ṽk,k -2α Ũk,k(11)
div.p: where, Ṽk,k and Ũk,k are the two body interaction terms, appearing with the coefficients which are respectively even and odd functions of α (see Appendix-B ). The first term in the above equation(Eq.( 11)) represents to an attractive interaction in the pairing mechanism while the second one gives a repulsive interaction. This energy expectation value W is a function of u k , v k and the parameter α. We need to minimize the total energy with respect to the Bogoliubov coefficients u k , v k as well as with respect to α.
The 'superconducting-gap' function is now defined as
div.formula: ∆ ( k) = ij V i,j c + i,↑ c + j,↓ CBCS(12)
div.p: where, V ij is the pairing amplitude which connects two lattice sites i and j and for simplicity, we consider the nearest neighbour coupling only. The expectation value in the above equation (Eq.( 12)) is evaluated over
div.formula: Ψ CBCS (see Appendix-C ).
div.p: On evaluating the paired operator expectation value and rearranging the odd and even powers of α in the local order parameter, we define two distinct 'gaps' as follows
div.formula: ∆ ( k) 2 = k 2α Ũkk u k v k 1 + 2α(α -1)|v k | 2(14)
div.p: The physical origin of these two gaps entirely depends on how the double occupancy is being prevented while calculating the pairing amplitude. The even power (here 0 and 2) of α only contributes when the pair operator picks up the electrons pair wise either from the ideal non-interacting FS ground state or from the double occupancy excluded CFS state. However, the contribution of the odd power of α enters in the same calculation while the pair operator acts simultaneously on the ideal FS part as well as the Coulomb correlated FS part. So, that calculation of odd α part, there is a mixing between ideal FS and partially double occupancy excluded CFS state. The pairing for the first case (see Eq.( 13)) is considered to be occurred very close regime to Fermi surface, while the same for the other gap is regarded deep inside the Fermi sea from the surface. In this context, Appendix-C is added hereby detailing the analytical expression of these two gap functions ∆ 1 and ∆ 2 . However, it is to be noted that, in the zero correlation regime (i.e. α = 0), ∆ 2 vanishes as expected and ∆ 1 becomes identical with the definition of ideal BCS gap (for 1-well) (see Eqs.( 13) and ( 14)).

div.head: B. Minimization of ground state energy with respect to both the pairing correlation and Coulomb correlation and self-consistent gap equations
div.p: The interplaying aspect of the Coulomb correlation and the phonon driven AEE interaction in this pairing mechanism is actively carried out variationally by following extremization with respect to both the Bogoliubov coefficients as well as the Gutzwiller parameter(α). For the time being, we take a parametric substitution of the Bogoliubov coefficients u k and v k as
div.formula: u k = sinθ k ; v k = cosθ k(15)
div.p: On incorporating above transformation, the ground state energy and the 'pairing-gaps' become
div.formula: W = k 2ε k 1 -2α + 2α 2 cos 2 θ k 1 + 2α(α -1)cos 2 θ k + 1 4 k,k P k,k sin2θ k sin2θ k [1 + 2α(α -1)cos 2 θ k ] [1 + 2α(α -1)cos 2 θ k ](16)
∆ 2 = 1 2 k 2α Ũkk sin2θ k [1 + 2α(α -1)cos 2 θ k ](18)
div.p: Hence, the total ground state energy W and two gaps ∆ 1 and ∆ 2 all three appear as a function of θ k and α. Therefore, the minimization condition with respect to both the Bogoliubov coefficients and the Gutzwiller parameter runs as follows
On solving these two simultaneous equations we obtain
div.formula: sin2θ k = (A -1)∆ k ε k + ∆ k ∆ 2 k (2A -1) + A 2 ε 2 k A (∆ 2 k + ε 2 k )(21
div.p: ) where, A = 1 -2α + 2α 2 . From the above equation, one can get the analytical expressions of the coefficients u k and v k and making use of them therefore one can obtain the quasi-particle energy. After carrying it out, we find that the quasi-particle energy is consistent with that of the BCS theory at zero correlation. Further, the energy gap at k = 0 increases with the rise in α value. Again, utilizing Eq.( 21), which is going to set the expressions of the Bogoliubov coefficients u k and v k , we obtain the self-consistent coupled gap equations for ∆ 1 and ∆ 2 as

These two equations are the self-consistent gap equations for ∆ 1 and ∆ 2 in which ∆ 1 (∆ 2 ) corresponds to the pairing amplitudes in the momentum state k whereas ∆ 1 (∆ 2 ) is that for the momentum state k . To set up these two equations, an assumption has been taken about the smallness of the amplitude of the gaps in comparison to the single particle energy, i.e. ∆i ε 1(i = 1, 2). Meanwhile, the effect of Coulomb correlation is now well established through the Gutzwiller parameter(α) in above two self-consistent gap equations(Eq.( 22) and ( 23)). Again, in the zero correlation regime, one can not get solution for the ∆ 2 as expected, whereas the other gap-equation becomes identical with the BCS gap-equation corresponding to the 1-well model. The self-consistent gap equations are now solved in the following section by specifying physically both the range of the pairing potential as well as the range of the momentum states for the pairing fermions.

div.head: C. Evaluation of the pairing gaps
div.p: We herewith follow the multi square-well prescription as proposed by Ginzburg and Kirzhnits to get the pairing gaps [44][45][46]. In their context, the screened Coulomb potential sets the repulsive square-well potential and the attractive pairing interaction originates from various boson exchange mechanisms. The bosonic mediators such as phonon, charge transfer-exciton, plasmon and magnon(or paramagnon) are mainly considered for pairing in parental metallic phase [47,48]. However, in our present work we here choose phonon as the bosonic mediator for the attractive pairing interaction. Several recently discovered superconductors belonging to the hydride and super-hydride family are known to be based upon the phonon exchange pairing mechanism, as pointed earlier [1][2][3][4][5][6].

div.head: Pairing gap within the attractive well:
div.p: The superconducting gap functions as in Eq.( 22) correspond to the correlated fermion pairing which is physically valid for the entire regimes of Coulomb correlation in terms of Gutzwiller parameter; α [i.e. 0 α 1]. However, this gap equation is identical to the noninteracting fermion pairing gap, as defined in the conventional BCS formalism in the absence of the Coulomb correlation (i.e. α = 0). In this pairing mechanism, pairing potential Ṽk,k plays the pivotal role and based on the nature of the bosonic mediator, we can set the interaction frequency regime of the attractive potential well. For the time being, we wish to study the phonon-mediated pairing and we set the upper cut-off frequency as the Debye frequency in the normal metallic phase i.e. ω c . Hence, the potential well is defined by following the BCS prescription as:
div.formula: Ṽkk = -V for -ω c ε k , ε k + ω c = 0 otherwise(24)
div.p: An important assumption, we took here is that with the case of isotropic pairing (s-wave) on the Fermi surface, ∆ 1 ∆ 1 with ω c E F [34]. The pairing gap ∆ 1 can now be evaluated by integrating out the right hand side of Eq.( 22) for all the possible momentum states close to the Fermi surface. Thus, one arrives at the following equation

where, Ω(ε k ) is the single spin electronic density of states(DOS) per unit volume(see fig: 2) and A = 1 -2α + 2α 2 . Since, our lattice system is a simple cubic(SC) lat-tice, we need to consider the single spin electron density of state(DOS) to evaluate the integral in Eq. [25].

The DOS for SC lattice from the TB dispersion relation is evaluated by using the elliptic integral of the first kind as shown in fig: 2; where, D = 6t is the half-width of the band with nearest neighbour hopping amplitude t [49,50]. Two Van Hove singularity points are there within the band (at ±D/3) where both real and imaginary part of the Green's function are continuous but their derivatives show discontinuity [51]. However, within these two singularity points the DOS is almost constant as expected near the Fermi level and hence it is approximated as Ω 0 .

The integral on the right hand side of Eq.( 25) is now evaluated within these two singularity points such that the range of the attractive well (see Eq.( 24)) does not exceed the limit, D 3 ω c . In this context, we take another approximation, that the pairing gap (here, ∆ 1 ) is much smaller in amplitude in comparison to the single-particle energy viz. ∆ ε 1. Further using this approximations and rearranging the integrand in Eq.( 25), we obtain
(26) where, the attractive coupling constant λ is the product of Ω 0 and V is defined as the dimensionless quantity as usual, i.e. λ = V Ω 0 .

The superconducting pairing gap, ∆ 1 is plotted against α for two different hopping amplitudes t = 0.25eV (fig: 3(a)) and t = 0.30eV (fig: 3(b)) with the range of the attractive potential ω c = 0.05eV in the weak coupling limit (λ 0.3). In the zero correlation regime (α = 0) it matches exactly with the BCS gap and for a finite coupling constant it decays with α. ∆ 1 however increases with the coupling strength for a given correlation value and the gap closes above a certain correlation value irrespective of the strength of the coupling constant. This gap vanishes for α = 0.605 (see fig: 3(a) and (b)). Beyond this critical value of α, the pairing correlation is no more possible within the attractive square-well and the system goes towards the Coulomb correlated normal state.

In fig: 4, the variation of pairing gap ∆ 1 with the coupling constant is shown for t = 0.25eV and t = 0.30eV (see fig: 4(a) and (b)). The color lines indicate different α values. It is however observed that ∆ 1 increases with the coupling strength for a given α value but the gap opens up beyond a finite threshold λ value for each α value. As expected, ∆ 1 gets suppressed with an increase in the magnitude of α.

div.head: Pairing gap within the repulsive well:
div.p: The second pairing gap function as in Eq.( 23), is somewhat different from the first one in terms of its origin as well as its physical nature and range of pairing potential. At zero correlation (α = 0), again this gap function vanishes as predicted(see Eq.( 23)) and later we will show that the gap opens up for the finite non-zero correlation value. However, the pairing potential Ũkk is repulsive in nature and pairing is taking place in quite an extended regime around the Fermi surface. Hereby, keeping in mind above two criteria, we define the range of the repulsive well as
div.formula: Ũkk = U for -ε F ε k , ε k +ε F = 0 otherwise (27) U > 0.
div.p: where, ε F is the Fermi energy. Again, we consider the case of isotropic pairing (s-wave) on the Fermi surface and we assume that ∆ 2 ∆ 2 . The pairing gap ∆ 2 can now be evaluated by integrating out the right hand side of Eq.( 23) for all the possible momentum states and following the form of potential well as in Eq.( 27). Hence, one can get the following form

However, the integration for the ∆ 2 is not that simple because of its very large frequency regime. However, the DOS for SC lattice shows a flat region around the Fermi level but beyond two singularity points it varies with single particle energy(see fig: 2). One can numerically evaluate the integration beyond the singularity points. Meanwhile, the integration is done over three different regimes of ε k , as:
div.formula: (i) -ε F ε k -ω c ; (ii) -ω c ε k + ω c ; and (iii) + ω c ε k +ε F .
div.p: Here, in fig: 5, variation of ∆ 2 with α is shown for t = 0.25eV and t = 0.30eV . In the weak Coulomb correlation regime, ∆ 2 is found to be zero and it opens up after certain α value. However, ∆ 2 increases slowly upto certain α value and then again it deceases with large α values. Ultimately, ∆ 2 vanishes as α approaches very strong correlation regime.

The above features may be understood as follows:-At lower values of α, a sizeable fraction of doubly occupied sites are allowed and therefore Cooper pairs can survive in the 2-well scenario; thereby leading to a finite magnitude of ∆ 2 with a weightage transfer from ∆ 1 to ∆ 2 as well, causing the growth of ∆ 2 with α. However, as α increases further to attain larger values, the doubly occupied sites get mostly eliminated leading to a fall of ∆ 2 . Figura 6: Variation of ∆ 1 and ∆ 2 with α for t = 0.25 superconducting pairing. However, our two-well model is quite different from our previous work in which we have studied the effect of the Coulomb correlation on paired state [34]. The non-trivial commutation relation among P G and P BCS makes these two superconducting pairing scenarios quite distinct. In other words, we can say that the two alternative routes through which we have introduced the Coulomb correlation in phonon mediated superconducting pairing mechanisms, are itself different from each other in terms of ordering the operators (i.e. P G and P BCS ) while defining the respective variational state. This leads to two very different consequences in the superconducting phases, as seen here and reported earlier in ref. [34].

For the earlier case, the parental state is a non-interacting ideal FS ground state, and pairing fermions are pair wise picked up from the uncorrelated FS state. The effect of the Coulomb correlation on the phonon mediated superconducting paired state is then studied on the BCS ground state. However, for the current scenario, the FS ground state of the normal phase itself is Coulomb correlated and the boson mediated superconducting pairing is then studied through the BCS pairing operator. The Coulomb correlation is therefore implemented in the parental normal state and the superconducting pairing then takes place in this correlated background. It is to be noted that both of the variational approaches discussed here and in ref. [34] are legitimate under different experimental conditions. In more detail, if one follows the path as in ref. [34], the Coulomb correlation on the paired state can be externally adjusted through the induced pressure or by a sample size-dependent correlation controller [1,2,35,52]. Some superconducting systems, such as heavy-fermion superconductors, have a strongly correlated parental normal state for which the superconducting pairing is largely affected by the strong Coulomb correlation [53,54]. In view of our present pairing scenario, as discussed here, one may follow these types of correlated systems for the experimental realization.

For the present case, we have variationally treated both the pairing correlation as well as the Coulomb correlation to set up the gap-equations and then the square wells are defined by following Ginzburg-Kirzhnits's prescription [44][45][46]. However, the double well structure appears here because of the origin of the pairing scheme as defined in sec-2.

In the meantime, ∆ 1 validates its physical appearance only within the effective attractive well whereas ∆ 2 is found within the potential well as in Eq. (27). At α = 0, the ratio of ∆1 ∆0 , (where ∆ 0 is the ideal BCS pairing gap at zero temperature) is 1 and ∆ 2 is reduced to zero. In other words, it can be said that if the Coulomb correlation is switched off, the system reduces to the ideal BCS model. For a finite coupling, ∆ 1 decays with α and beyond α = 0.605 it reduces to zero (see fig: 6(a)). This critical α value decreases as the λ value is gradually reduced(see fig: 3(a) and (b)). However, for a given α value, ∆ 1 rises in magnitude with the increase in λ. In summary of our study on the pairing scenario in correlated fermions on the simple cubic lattice, we have shown the characteristic behaviour of ∆ 1 and ∆ 2 with α (see fig: 6(b)). Both the pairing-gaps are found to be consistent throughout the entire physically applicable regime of the Coulomb correlation parameter (i.e. 0 α 1). In terms of amplitudes, ∆ 2 is seen to be much smaller than ∆ 1 . However, all the analytical outcomes reported here are on the basis of FL description of the parental normal phase. The Gutzwiller projection scheme has been implemented to set up CBCS variational state with α as a variational parameter and following the constraint 0 α 1. In this context, there should be a critical α value beyond which FL description of the parental normal phase itself may not work. For the time being, this limiting criterion is under investigation and in future, we will report it separately.
Apêndice A: Normalization of CBCS state Normalization of the CBCS state is carried out here with some major steps as
div.formula: Ψ CBCS |Ψ CBCS = |k | k F k l |k| k F k l F S| 1 -αn l ↑ n l ↓   u k + v k f g e ik .(r f -r g ) C g ↓ C f ↑     u k + v k f g e -ik.(r f -rg) C + f ↑ C + g↓   (1 -αn l↑ n l↓ ) |F S (A1)
div.p: The orthogonality criterion of the state eliminates most of the off-diagonal terms and it is obvious that only those terms with equal number of fermion creation and annihilation operators will contribute with a non-zero value to the normalization. Hence, the contributing terms are
div.formula: Ψ CBCS |Ψ CBCS = |k | k F k l |k| k F k l [u k u k + f ,g f,g v k v k e k .r f g e k.r f g F S|C g ↓ C f ↑ C + f ↑ C + g↓ |F S -α f ,g f,g v k v k e k .r f g e k.r f g F S|n l ↑ n l ↓ C g ↓ C f ↑ C + f ↑ C + g↓ |F S -α f ,g f,g v k v k e k .r f g e k.r f g F S|C g ↓ C f ↑ C + f ↑ C + g↓ n l↑ n l↓ |F S + α 2 f ,g f,g v k v k e k .r f g e k.r f g F S|n l ↑ n l ↓ C g ↓ C f ↑ C + f ↑ C + g↓ n l↑ n l↓ |F S ] + α 2 k F k,k f ,g f,g v k v k e k .r f g e k.r f g F S|(n 1↑ n 1↓ n 2↑ n 2↓ + ......... + n (N -1)↑ n (N -1)↓ n N ↑ n N ↓ )C g ↓ C f ↑ C + f ↑ C + g↓ |F S (A2)
div.p: where, r f g = r f -r g and r f g = r f -r g .
div.formula: Ψ CBCS |Ψ CBCS = |k | k F k l |k| k F k l [u k u k + f ,g f,g v k v k e ik .r f g e -ik.r f g δ kk δ f f δ gg -α f ,g f,g v k v k e ik .r f g e -ik.r f g δ kk δ l f δ l g δ f f δ gg -α f ,g f,g v k v k e ik .r f g e -ik.r f g δ kk δ lf δ lg δ f f δ gg +α 2 f ,g f,g v k v k e ik .r f g e -ik.r f g δ kk δ l f δ l g δ f f δ gg ] + α 2 k F k,k f ,g f,g v k v k e ik .r f g e -ik.r f g (δ kk δ f f δ gg + ......) (A3)
div.p: This calculation is carried out for the momentum state k = k and using the properties of Kronecker delta function we arrange the contributing terms as a coefficient of the power of α as :
In a compact form this normalization constant
div.formula: Ψ CBCS |Ψ CBCS = k 1 -2α(1 -α)|v k | 2 [since, |u k | 2 + |v k | 2 = 1] (A5)
div.p: Apêndice B: Ground state energy
Total ground state energy is calculated as
div.formula: W = Ψ CBCS |H|Ψ CBCS Ψ CBCS |Ψ CBCS (B1)
div.p: The variational state and the Hamiltonian are as defined in the main text(see Eq.( 6) and ( 8)). However, this is also evaluated in a similar manner as we did for the case of the normalization constant.
div.formula: W = 1 CBCS Ψ|Ψ CBCS |k| k F k |k| k F k N l N l F S|(1 -αn l ↑ n l ↓ )[u k + v k f g e ik .(r f -r g ) C g ↓ C f ↑ ]   i,j 2ε ij b + i b j + i ,j V i j b + i b j   [u k + v k f g e -ik.(r f -rg) C + f ↑ C + g↓ ] (1 -αn l↑ n l↓ ) |F S = 1 CBCS Ψ|Ψ CBCS [ |k| k F k |k| k F k F S|[1 -α(n 1↑ n 1↓ + ......) + α 2 (n 1↑ n 1↓ n 2↑ n 2↓ + ....) + ...] (u k + v k f g e ik .(r f -r g ) C g ↓ C f ↑ )   i,j 2ε ij b + i b j + i ,j V i j b + i b j   (u k + v k f g e -ik.(r f -rg) C + f ↑ C + g↓ )(1 -α(n 1↑ n 1↓ + ......) + α 2 (n 1↑ n 1↓ n 2↑ n 2↓ + ....) + ...)|F S ] = 1 CBCS Ψ|Ψ CBCS [ k F k k F k [ F S|(u k + v k f g e ik.r f g C g ↓ C f ↑ )   i,j 2ε ij b + i b j + i ,j V i j b + i b j   (u k + v k f g e -ik.(r f -rg) C + f ↑ C + g↓ )|F S ] -α[ |k| k F k |k| k F k [ F S|(n 1↑ n 1↓ + n 2↑ n 2↓ ......) (u k + v k f g e ik.r f g C g ↓ C f ↑ )   i,j 2ε ij b + i b j + i ,j V i j b + i b j   (u k + v k f g e -ik.(r f -rg) C + f ↑ C + g↓ )|F S ] + h.c.] +α 2 [ |kk F k k F k [ F S|(n 1↑ n 1↓ n 2↑ n 2↓ + ......)(u k + v k f g e ik.r f g C g ↓ C f ↑ )   i,j 2ε ij b + i b j + i ,j V i j b + i b j   (u k + v k f g e -ik.(r f -rg) C + f ↑ C + g↓ )|F S ] + h.c.]]](B2)
div.p: Herewith, we arrange the coefficients of the power of the Gutzwiller parameter; α as :
div.formula: α 0 : 2 k ε k |v k | 2 1 + 2α(α -1)|v k | 2 + kk Ṽkk u k u k v k v k [1 + 2α(α -1)|v k | 2 ][1 + 2α(α -1)|v k | 2 ] α 1 : -4 k ε k |v k | 2 1 + 2α(α -1)|v k | 2 -2 kk Ũkk u k u k v k v k [1 + 2α(α -1)|v k | 2 ][1 + 2α(α -1)|v k | 2 ] α 2 : 4 k ε k |v k | 2 1 + 2α(α -1)|v k | 2 + kk Ṽkk u k u k v k v k [1 + 2α(α -1)|v k | 2 ][1 + 2α(α -1)|v k | 2 ] (B3)
div.p: while evaluating the ground state energy we find that ε k = ij ε ij e ik.rij , with r ij = r i -r j ; and the connecting matrix elements are
div.formula: Ṽkk = i j V i j e i(k .r i -k.r j ) (B4) Ũkk = - i j V i j e i(k .r i -k.r j ) (B5)
div.p: Here, Ṽkk and Ũkk are looking similar but they connect the momentum states from two different regimes. We follow the Overhauser formalism to define those regimes [17]. For Ṽkk , the values of k and k lie within the range k F < k, k < k c ; where, the momentum state k c corresponds to the energy state ω c . In a similar way, for Ũkk , the values for both k and k satisfy k F < k, k < k max ; where k max corresponds to an energy state ∼ 2ε F . It is interesting to note that if we put α = 0, only Ṽkk is present in that calculation. This is identical to the interacting two body potential as is found in the BCS model. The first part of each of the above set of coefficients(Eq.(B3)), signifies the single particle hopping energy. In a similar way, the second part of these coefficients is the contribution coming from the interacting potential term. In the main text, we use a compact expression for the potential energy term as:
div.formula: kk [(1 + α 2 Ṽkk ) -2α Ũkk ]u k u k v k v k [1 + 2α(α -1)|v k | 2 ][1 + 2α(α -1)|v k | 2 ] = kk P kk u k u k v k v k [1 + 2α(α -1)|v k | 2 ][1 + 2α(α -1)|v k | 2 ] (B6)
div.p: Apêndice C: Superconducting pairing gaps
The superconducting-pairing gap function corresponding to the CBCS state is calculated as follows :
div.formula: ∆ ( k) = ij V ij c + i↑ c + j↓ CBCS (C1)
div.p: Our system is a simple cubic lattice and for simplicity, we consider the nearest neighbour pairing only.

On evaluating the pair-operator expectation value on the CBCS state we have, In this calculation, those terms containing upto α 2 will contribute and the terms with higher powers in α will be eliminated because of the Pauli's exclusion principle. Moreover, on rearranging the contributing terms we find that: For α 0 term : where, the upper cutoff of the k is k c ; k c corresponds to the maximum accessible momentum state above the Fermi sea in terms of Debye energy of the system(i.e. ω c ). The phonon mediated superconducting pairing is taken into account through the s-wave channel in the 2-square well scenario. The attractive pairing interaction takes place very close regime to the Fermi surface. Evaluating the pairing amplitude, we find that all the contributions coming from the even power in α s (i.e. 0 and 2) belong to the attractive pairing interaction. Therefore, the product over k and k for that calculation is running for all those momentum states which are close to the Fermi surface, i.e. δk = |k F -k c |.
div.formula: ∆ = 1 CBCS Ψ|Ψ CBCS [ |k | k F k |k| k F k N l N l F S|(1 -αn l ↑ n l ↓ )[u k + v k f g e ik .(r f -r g ) C g ↓ C f ↑ ]   ij V ij C + i↑ C + j↓   [u k + v k f g e -ik.(r f -rg) C + f ↑ C + g↓ ] (1 -αn l↑ n l↓ ) |F S ] = |k | k F k |k| k F k F S|[1 -α(n 1↑ n 1↓ + ......) + α 2 (n 1↑ n 1↓ n 2↑ n 2↓ + ....) + ...] (u k + v k f g e ik .(r f -r g ) C g ↓ C f ↑ )   ij V ij C + i↑ C + j↓   (u k + v k f g e -ik.(r f -rg) C + f ↑ C + g↓ ) (1
F S|1.v k 1 f g e ik 1 .r f g C g ↓ C f ↑ ( ij V ij C + i↑ C + j↓ ).
div.p: For a given a momentum state k 1 = k 1 ∼ k F , and i = f ; j = g one can obtain the first term in the left hand side of the above equation(Eq.(C2)) and later on adding the same for all other momentum states close to the Fermi surface, we get the summed up result as in the right side of the above equation. In a similar way, the contribution of α 2 term :
div.formula: F S|(n 1↑ n 1↓ n 2↑ n 2↓ + ...).v k 1 f g e ik 1 .r f g C g ↓ C f ↑ ( ij V ij C + i↑ C + j↓ ).u k1 .1|F S + ... = kk Ṽkk u k v k (C5)
div.p: The contribution of α 1 term : This odd α contributing term appears as follows
div.formula: -2 F S|1.v k 1 f g e ik 1 .r f g C g ↓ C f ↑ ( ij V ij C + i↑ C + j↓ ).u k1 .1|CF S + ....... = 2 kk Ũkk u k v k (C6)
div.p: where, for a given momentum state from the ket state k,
div.formula: Ũkk = - ij
div.p: V ij e i(k .ri-k.rj ) (C7)

here, the highest accessible momentum state for k is k max , which can correspond to an energy state up to ∼ 2ε F . In this case, those momentum states that enter into the pairing amplitude calculation are simultaneously coming from both the ideal non-interacting FS state as well as the CFS state. The CFS state still carries a weightage of allowed doubly occupied sites but to access all those states we have to consider a wide range of k values starting from the deep inside the Fermi sea (k = 0) to well above the Fermi surface (∼ 2k F ) as well. Earlier, we had also followed this criterion to define the repulsive potential well for the Ũkk (see Eq.( 27) in the main text). Therefore, the contribution of all three terms are:
div.formula: α 0 : kk Ṽkk u k v k 1 + 2α(α -1)|v k | 2 (C8) α 1 : kk Ũkk u k v k 1 + 2α(α -1)|v k | 2 (C9) α 0 : 2 kk Ṽkk u k v k 1 + 2α(α -1)|v k | 2 (C10)
div.p: Therefore, the two gaps are now defined as
div.formula: ∆ 1 = k Ṽkk u k v k (1 + 2α 2 ) 1 + 2α(α -1)|v k | 2 (C11) ∆ 2 = k α Ũkk u k v k 1 + 2α(α -1)|v k | 2 (C12)

figure.figDesc: . The pair operators, b + i and b i are now in real space written as b + i = k e k.ri b + k and b i = k e -k.ri b k , where the Cooper pair operators are b

figure.head: Figura 2 :
figure.figDesc: Figura 2: The Electronic Density of state(DOS) for simple cubic lattice.

figure.head: Figura 3 :
figure.figDesc: Figura 3: Variation of ∆ 1 with α for different coupling values

figure.head: Figura 4 :
figure.figDesc: Figura 4: Variation of ∆ 1 with λ for different coupling values
u k1 .1|F S + ....... = kk Ṽkk u k v k (C3)for a given momentum state from the ket state k(∼ k F ),Ṽkk = ij V ij e i(k .ri-k.rj ) (C4)

div.head: ACKNOWLEDGMENTS
div.p: KM thanks UGC, India for the financial support [ref. no. 522495(2016)].

monogr.title: Proc. R. Soc, London Ser. A

analytic.title: Phase diagram of the two-dimensional Hubbard-Holstein model

Effect of Correlation on the Ferromagnetism of Transition Metals
Correlation of Electrons in a Narrow s Band
Analytic calculation of ground-state properties of correlated fermions with the Gutzwiller wave function
Gutzwiller correlated wave functions in fi-nite dimensions d: A systematic expansion in 1/d
The Gutzwiller approximation for degenerate bands: a formal derivation

analytic.title: Gutzwiller variational theory for the Hubbard model with attractive interaction
Gutzwiller variational wave function for multi-orbital Hubbard models in finite dimensions
The resonating valence bond state in La2CuO4 and superconductivity

analytic.title: Studies of the spin diffusion coefficient and the spin stiffness constant for the t-J model on lowdimensional lattices and possible application to doped antiferromagnets

analytic.title: Superconductivity in metastable simple cubic alloys
Fermi-surface-brillouin-zone interaction in simple cubic Te-Au alloys
Superconductivity at the highest transition temperature of 8.1 K in a simple cubic AuxSb1-x-yT ey alloy system synthesized under high pressure

The problem of high-temperature superconductivity
Confinement-Engineered Superconductor to Correlated-Insulator Transition in a van der Waals Monolayer
Superconductivity in the presence of strong Pauli paramagnetism:CeCu2Si2
